coredata.dc:title: A novel benzo[a]phenazin-based fluorescence probe for selective detection of cysteine with anti-cancer potency
coredata.dc:creator: Rui, Bing
Feng, Yangrui
Luo, Lan
coredata.dc:description: Among the physiological and pathological sulfur-containing species, cysteine (Cys) is the most typical one which is an important component of the REDOX system in vivo. Monitoring the level of Cys from other competing species seems quite important in pre-clinical diagnosis and therapeutic evaluation. Herein, we developed a selective fluorescent probe, BPCys, for Cys from the benzo[a]phenazin backbone which had the potential of anti-cancer potency. BPCys suggested advantages including high specificity (40 fold over other species), high sensitivity (detection limit: 18 nM), wide pH adaptability (6.0–11.0) and in particular, the anti-cancer effect. Biological assays and in silico simulation hinted the potency of the detecting product on Topoisomerase I/II. In brief, this study raised a practical strategy for monitoring the Cys level in living cells, especially in cancer models with its anti-cancer potential, thus opened the mind of exploring more specific tool for specific applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine detection
Fluorescent probe
Benzo[a]phenazin moiety
High selectivity
Anti-cancer potency

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Shematic illustration of developing benzo[a]phenazin-based fluorescence probe for selective detection of cysteine with anti-cancer potency.

(a) The variation of the fluorescence emission spectra of BPCys with various Cys concentrations (0–50 equiv.); (b) The correlation between fluorescence intensity at 480 nm and the Cys concentrations; Inner: The linear fitting analysis (Linear range: 0–8 equiv.).

The intensity ratio (F1/F0 at 480 nm) values of BPCys towards various analytes. (a, b) BPCys: 10 μM, Cys: 100 μM, Hcy: 1 mM; GSH: 1 mM; S2−: 100 μM, SO3 2−: 100 μM, HSO3− : 100 μM, SO4 2−: 1 mM; H2S2: 1 mM; PhSH: 1 mM, H2O2: 100 μM, Other: 1 mM. F1: BPCys with analytes; F0: BPCys only.
The binding maps of the compounds into TOP I/TOP II. (a, b) CPT, BPCys and BPCys-Prod into TOP I; (c, d) Etoposide, BPCys and BPCys-Prod into TOP II.

The confocol images of HeLa cells incubated with BPCys in different conditions. In the control group (a–c): the cells were directly incubated with BPCys (10 μM) for 15 min at 37 °C; In other groups, the cells were initially treated with NEM (2 mM, 30 min) and BPCys (10 μM, 15 min), then incubated with (d–f) the buffer, (g–i) 100 μM Cys or (j–l) 200 μM Cys for another 30 min at 37 °C. Green channel: 450–570 nm; Scale bar: 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Cytotoxcity of the probe BPCys and the detecting product BPCys-Prod.
The Topoisomerase I/II inhibition activity of the probe BPCys and the detecting product BPCys-Prod.

head.ce:title: A novel benzo[a]phenazin-based fluorescence probe for selective detection of cysteine with anti-cancer potency

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Life Sciences, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Life Sciences, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among the physiological and pathological sulfur-containing species, cysteine (Cys) is the most typical one which is an important component of the REDOX system in vivo. Monitoring the level of Cys from other competing species seems quite important in pre-clinical diagnosis and therapeutic evaluation. Herein, we developed a selective fluorescent probe, BPCys, for Cys from the benzo[a]phenazin backbone which had the potential of anti-cancer potency. BPCys suggested advantages including high specificity (40 fold over other species), high sensitivity (detection limit: 18 nM), wide pH adaptability (6.0–11.0) and in particular, the anti-cancer effect. Biological assays and in silico simulation hinted the potency of the detecting product on Topoisomerase I/II. In brief, this study raised a practical strategy for monitoring the Cys level in living cells, especially in cancer models with its anti-cancer potential, thus opened the mind of exploring more specific tool for specific applications.

We developed a selective fluorescent probe, BPCys, for Cys from the benzo[a]phenazin backbone which had the potential of anti-cancer potency. BPCys suggested advantages including high specificity, high sensitivity, wide pH adaptability and the anti-cancer effect. Biological assays and in silico simulation hinted the potency of the detecting product on Topoisomerase I/II. In brief, this work raised a practical strategy for monitoring the Cys level in living cells, especially in cancer models with its anti-cancer potential, thus opened the mind of exploring more specific tool for specific applications.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Within the physiological and pathological procedures of organism, the circulatory system of sulfur element seems an indispensable component [1,2], which contains the conformations of sulfur dioxide (SO2), hydrogen sulfide (H2S), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), thiophenol (PhSH) and so on according to specific oxido-reduction [3–8]. Cys, as one of the sulfur-containing species, is typical and significant in both composition and function [9,10]. It is commonly generated from serine and methionine by methionine adenosyltransferase, cystathionine-β-synthase and cystathionine-γ-lyase [11–13]. The physiological level of Cys is 30–200 μM in human [14], while the abnormal Cys level is connected with cardiovascular diseases, neurological disorders, liver damage, and growth retardation [15–18]. In tumor, high GSH level (over 1 mM and even up to 10 mM) could significantly cause a consequent increase in endogenous Cys level [19,20]. Thus, considering concentration is essential for revealing key interactions and parameters in pre-clinical diagnose [21].

There are a number of methods currently used to detect cysteine including high performance liquid chromatography [22], mass spectrometry [23], capillary electrophoresis [24], and fluorescent probes which brought the pre-clinical convenience from high cost and sophisticated operations [25]. With various fluorophores, the Cys-recognition groups involved N-ethylmaleimide [26], chloracetate ester [27], α,β-unsaturated ketone [28], nitrobenzoxadiazole [29], and of course the most applicative acrylate [30–46]. Acrylate usually act as the pioneer during the attemption of novel ideas such as sulobility optimization [47] and optical regulation [48]. Here we attempted to develop a selective fluorescent probe for Cys with anti-cancer potency, thus to initial the linkage between diagnostic and therapeutic concerns. There seemed several logistic requirements: 1) high selectivity for detecting target (Cys here) in the scene (tumor cells here); 2) the detecting procedure which was beneficial for the therapeutic purpose; 3) the therapeutic strategy which was free from interfering the detecting accuracy. All of them have been rigorously validated.

Among the fluorophores, benzo[a]phenazin (“BP” for short in this work) moiety was reported to show anti-cancer potency [49,50]. This kind of small molecules performed anti-proliferation effect on cancer cells via nucleic acid-related mechanisms. Different from interrupting protein-protein interactions [49], inhibiting Topoisomerase I/II was a more determined mechanism with derived substrates and established evaluating protocol [50]. Accordingly, benzo[a]phenazin could act as an anti-cancer agent.

In this work, we selected benzo[a]phenazin backbone to act as the origin of fluorophore due to its reported optical properties as well as anti-cancer potency [49,50]. The obtained probe, BPCys, was designed to fulfill the requirements above (Fig. 1 ). Acrylate was anchored to BP to realize the high selectivity. While the anti-cancer potential of the detecting procedure was investigated, the interference onto the accuracy of the detection was preliminarily evaluated. Therefore, a practical strategy for monitoring the Cys level in living cells, especially in cancer models with its anti-cancer potency, was established. To our knowledge, for small molecular fluorescent probes, this might be the first trial to combine the selective detection with anti-cancer potency.

For chemicals from commercial sources, they were used directly with no further purification. NMR data were from Bruker DRX-600 spectrometer, while Mass data were from Agilent 6540 UHD Accurate Mass Q-TOF LC/MS. The pH values were determined by PHS-25 pH-meter. The fluorescence-associated experiments were performed on Hitachi F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The biological imaging was performed on Olympus FV1000 confocal fluorescent microscope.

The probe BPCys was prepared in the stock solution of 1.0 mM in DMSO before the optical tests. The dilution was conducted with the buffer (PBS, 10 mM) and deionized water. Except for pointing out, the working concentration of BPCys was set as 10 μM. The concentration of Cys was set as 10-fold (100 μM) and that of other analytes was 100-fold (1 mM). The instrumental parameters included 5 nm excitation/emission slit widths, 600 V photomultiplier voltage, and the 410 nm excitation wavelength.

MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the probe and the detecting product with human epithelial cervical cancer cell line (HeLa), human breast adenocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7), human non-small-cell lung cancer cell line (A549), human colon cancer cell line (HT29) and human embryonic kidney cells (HEK293T). Colchicine was used as the positive control. After checking the OD (optical density) values at 490 nm, 510 nm and 550 nm, the values at 490 nm were used for the tests in consideration of both avoidance of interference and convenience for further calculation. The results were presented as half inhibitory concentration (IC50). All experiments were in triplicate.

The Topoisomerase I/II inhibitory activity was tested according to the reference [50]. Camptothecin (CPT) and Etoposide were used as controls, respectively. The percentages of the inhibition on the supercoiling were recorded. The inhibition of topoisomerase I/II by low concentration (20 μM) and high concentration (100 μM) of the compounds was tested.

All experiments were performed in accordance with the guidelines of National Medical Products Administration (P. R. China). All cell lines were cultured and sub-cultured in accordance with the guidelines of National Science Council (P. R. China).

Molecular docking simulation was conducted by Discovery Studio 3.1 (BIOVIA) and visualized on Discovery Studio Visualizer Client. The protein crystal structures of Topoisomerase I (PDB ID: 1TL8) and Topoisomerase II (PDB ID: 4G0U) were chosen from the RSCB Protein Data Bank. The protocol was set according to the reference [51].

BPCys was acquired as shown in Fig. 2. The characteristics were confirmed by satisfactory spectroscopic data (1H NMR, 13C NMR and HRMS, Figs. S1–6, Supporting Information (SI)). Notably, potential substitutes can be introduced from either reactant if necessary. According to the differences in HRMS spectra of BPCys and the detecting product BPCys-Prod, the responding mechanism could be indicated.

In preliminary tests, the probe BPCys indicated a negligible fluorescence at 525 nm when being excited at 430 nm (Figs. S7a and SI). After being treated with Cys, the peak of the emission band moved to 480 nm (Figs. S7b and SI) and increased dramatically when the excitation wavelength was set at 410 nm. Then we set the general detecting system as 10 μM BPCys with 100 μM Cys in PBS buffer (10 mM PBS, pH 7.4, 5% DMSO v/v) at 37 °C. Next, we tested the pH tolerance of the detection system. Within the range of 3.0–12.0, the detecting system exhibited steady fluorescence intensity within 6.0–10.0 (Figs. S8 and SI). Such a pH window inferred that BPCys could be applied in most of the physiological and pathological environments in human. For time conditions, BPCys could respond to Cys within 10 min. Therefore we set the incubation time as 15 min in later experiments. Besides, when the system was excited 12 h later, the fluorescence intensity showed no obvious decrease (Figs. S9 and SI).

The sensitivity of BPCys towards Cys was evaluated according to the fluorescence spectra of BPCys with various concentrations of Cys (Fig. 3 ). Along with the increase of added Cys (0–50 equiv.), the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm also enhanced and came to the equilibrium at 10.0 equivalent. The linear range was 1–8.0 equiv, and the fitting model was reliable with a Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.9989. After the substitution of correponding values, the Limit of Detection (LOD) of Cys was determined as 18 nM (0.0018 equiv.) via the formula 3σ/k (σ: background noise of 25 times, 0.035; k: slope value, 57.93). Thus, the sensitivity of BPCys was capable of monitoring Cys at physiological levels.

As mentioned above, the logistic requirements of diagnostic and therapeutic linkage included specificity, beneficiality and noninterference. Specificity was the initial one before the evaluation in living cancer cells. The related competing species, especially the sulfur-containing ones, were included to check the specificity of BPCys towards Cys (Fig. 4 a). The concentration of Cys was set as 100 μM (10 equiv.), while the concentrations of other species were set as their superior limit of physiological concentrations (Hcy: 1 mM; GSH: 1 mM; S2−: 100 μM) or higher level (SO3 2−: 100 μM, HSO3− : 100 μM, SO4 2−: 1 mM; H2S2: 1 mM; PhSH: 1 mM, H2O2: 100 μM). The broadened evaluation included 1 mM of amino acids (Ala, Arg, Asn, Asp, Gln, Glu, Gly, His, Ile, Leu, Lys, Met, Phe, Pro, Ser, Thr, Trp, Tyr, Val) (Fig. 4b). The results indicated that after the 15 min-incubation, only Cys could cause obvious enhancement of fluorescence intensity in the probe-containing system. Taken together, the high specificity of BPCys which built the assumption of further experiments was confirmed.

The cytotoxicity of the probe BPCys and the detecting product BPCys-Prod was evaluated with MTT assay. In this section, we chose four cancer cell lines including human epithelial cervical cancer cell line (HeLa), human breast adenocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7), human non-small-cell lung cancer cell line (A549), human colon cancer cell line (HT29) and one human normal cell line human embryonic kidney cells (HEK293T). As shown in Table 1 , we found that BPCys-Prod inferred broad anti-tumor effect upon the tested cancer cell lines (IC50 values around 10 μM), whereas the probe BPCys did not indicate obvious cytotoxcity (IC50 values > 60 μM for all cell lines, much higher than the working concentration 10 μM). This result indicated that the detection process itself had anti-cancer potency. Although this feature seemed conflict with the benefits of physiological monitoring, it seemed profitable for the detection in the diagnostic and therapeutic linkage.

According to the basic structure [50], the anti-cancer potency might be realized via the inhibition of Topoisomerase I/II. Therefore the enzymatic inhibition of the probe and detecting product was checked with CPT (inhibitor of TOP I) and Etoposide (inhibitor of TOP II) as controls. As shown in Table 2 , the probe itself did not show obvious TOP I/II inhibitory activity, but the detecting product exhibited considerable potency against both enzymes. This point was favorable on account that the TOPs are at the downstream of Cys-related DNA methylation in a relatively independent manner. Thus in the diagnostic and therapeutic linkage, the detecting target would not be notably interfered by the anti-cancer potency.

To visualize the possible binding patterns, we conducted the molecular docking with the probe BPCys, the product BPCys-Prod and the controls (CPT and Etoposide) into the binding site of both TOPI (PDB ID: 1TL8) and TOP II (PDB ID: 4G0U). The energy values of the binding patterns between the ligands and TOP I/II were displayed in Tables S1 and SI. The result agreed with that of the inhibitory assay. BPCys-Prod showed potential for both TOP I and TOP II, and seemed more close to CPT according to the energy values. The probe, BPCys, indicated slight binding ability, which agreed with its low cytotoxicity. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , both BPCys and BPCys-Prod seemed to bind into the similar sites of the controls, respectively. The enlarged partial views indicated that BPCys and BPCys-Prod were more similar to CPT (Fig. 5b) than to Etoposide (Fig. 5d) according to the overlapped parts of backbones. When we looked into the binding patterns of these molecules (Figs. S10 and SI), we found the differences between the probe and the others. In spite of showing possible interactions with key residues of the enzymes (CPT in TOP I: Lys532, Asp533; BPCys-Prod in TOP I: Lys532, Asn722; Etoposide in TOP II: Pro455, Arg503, Ala521, Glu522; BPCys-Prod in TOP II: Arg503), the probe BPCys mainly interacted with the alternative DNA/RNA chains. This result was also consistent with the previous enzymatic inhibitory tests. Theoretically, the anti-cancer potency was triggered along with the detecting procedure, which also guaranteed the safety of the probe itself.

We consulted the cytotoxicity results and tested the biological imaging performance of BPCys in HeLa cells under the confocal microscopy (Fig. 6 ). Since the endogenous Cys level was around 100 μM, as shown in Fig. 6a–c, the tested cells indicated obvious green fluorescence after being treated with 10 μM BPCys for 15 min. However, as shown in Fig. 6d–f, when we pre-treated the cells with the biothiols scavenger N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 2 mM, 30 min), the further incubation with 10 μM BPCys for 15 min could not lead to the signal enhancement in the green channel. After the clearance of biothiols by NEM, further addition of cysteine (100 μM in Fig. 6g–i or 200 μM, Fig. 6j-l) and incubation could still exhibit the fluorescence enhancement in a concentration-dependent manner. Taken together, these data suggested that BPCys was a practical implement for the biological imaging of Cys in living cells.

To summarize, from the benzo[a]phenazin backbone showing the potential of anti-cancer potency, we developed a specifically fluorescent probe BPCys. Compared with the most recent reports (Tables S2 and SI), the basic advantages of this probe included high specificity (40 fold over other species), high sensitivity (detection limit: 18 nM), wide pH adaptability (6.0–11.0). In particular, BPCys indicated the anti-cancer effect during the detection procedure, which was possibly caused by the affection upon Topoisomerase I/II as hinted by the biological assays and in silico simulation. As for the Topoisomerase inhibition, it was not common among all the investigated cases. This kind of structure-based anti-cancer potential initiated the linkage between diagnostic and therapeutic concerns. Briefly speaking, this work introduced an implement for monitoring the Cys level in living cells, especially in cancer models, which opened the mind of exploring more specific tool for specific applications.
Bing Rui: Methodology, Writing - Original Draft, Validation; Yangrui Feng: Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization; Lan Luo: Conceptualization, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Dr. Zhimin Yin for helpful discussions and critical reading of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121902.

S.C. Lu, Regulation of glutathione synthesis, Mol. Aspect. Med.. 30 (2009) 42-59.
